comments.author: quangdang46
comments.text:
## Expanded Scope from Security Governance
Treat this issue as the canonical home for policy semantics that control retention compliance, legal hold, deletion guarantees, redaction-aware forgetting, and policy precedence.

### Why this matters
The repo thesis explicitly forbids silent destructive behavior and requires provenance preservation. That means forgetting, archiving, suppression, redaction, soft delete, and hard delete cannot be discussed only as storage operations; they are policy outcomes.

### Source map
- docs/SECURITY_GOVERNANCE.md
- docs/MEMORY_MODEL.md
- docs/PLAN.md

### Future-Self Notes
- Distinguish utility-driven forgetting from compliance deletion.
- Legal hold must override ordinary forgetting or deletion flows.
- Deletion guarantees and redaction semantics should be explicit enough that interfaces can explain what was hidden, suppressed, archived, or actually removed.
- When policy rules conflict, this issue should define precedence rather than letting each interface guess.

title: Governance: define namespace enforcement and cross-namespace isolation rules
description:
Specify namespace defaults, validation before expensive retrieval, redaction and denial behavior across interfaces, and the rules that preserve cross-namespace isolation while still supporting approved sharing flows.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Default, missing, malformed, and cross-namespace request behavior is explicit.
- Policy denials and redactions preserve safety without leaking protected details and remain explainable to users.
- Retrieval, interface, and sharing work can depend on one stable namespace contract rather than re-deciding it per surface.

The project invariant is that namespace isolation is checked before expensive retrieval work. That only holds if the request contract and storage/retrieval layers share one common vocabulary for actor identity, scope, policy pinning, and denial/redaction behavior.

- Cross-agent sharing work must depend on this contract instead of bypassing it.
- Denial behavior must avoid leaking protected details through explain or error paths.

comments.author: quangdang46
comments.text:
## Failure Taxonomy from Failure Playbook
Use this issue as the canonical operator-facing map for at least these named incident classes:
- Tier1 overflow
- Tier2 index drift
- Tier3 segment corruption
- contradiction masking
- false association
- duplicate storms
- planner budget blow-up
- graph fanout explosion
- repair backlog growth
- latency regression
- cross-namespace leakage
- retention-policy bug

### Operational intent
For each class, define:
1. symptoms and early warning signals,
2. first response and degraded-mode promises,
3. rollback or stop-work triggers,
4. durable-evidence validation steps, and
5. post-repair validation.

### Future-Self Notes
The playbook is repetitive, but the repetition is a signal: preserve forensic logs, isolate scope when necessary, stop destructive jobs first, and compare derived state against durable evidence before trusting repaired outputs.

`docs/CACHE_AND_PREFETCH.md`, `docs/INDEXING_STRATEGIES.md`, and `docs/OPERATIONS.md` all reinforce the same design goal: caches and indexes may be valuable, but they must remain droppable, rebuildable, and subordinate to durable evidence.

- Derived accelerators must have invalidation and rebuild stories, not just performance wins.

title: Lifecycle subtask: define transition guards, failure semantics, and repair-task handoff
description:
Make transition guards, failed-transition behavior, prior-state preservation, and repair-task handoff explicit across core memory objects and subsystem-controlled lifecycle changes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Lifecycle transitions name the required guards, forbidden transitions, and the system behavior when a transition cannot complete.
- Failed transitions preserve prior state, emit explicit error or audit artifacts, and hand off repairable follow-up work instead of silently mutating state.
- Testing, repair, and governance work can depend on one canonical transition-failure contract.

title: Lifecycle state-machine subtask: define repair-queue handoff and retry boundaries

- Recovery and resilience tests can consume this contract directly.

## Expanded Explainability Scope for Cache and Routing Visibility
Inspect and explain surfaces should expose not only score decomposition and policy filters, but also cache participation and routing visibility.

### Include at minimum
- cache hit or miss and cache family used,
- stale-result warnings or freshness markers,
- prefetch or warmup contribution when relevant,
- why a cache or tier was skipped, bypassed, or disabled,
- candidate counts before and after major routing stages.

### Why this matters
Without this, operators will misdiagnose cache staleness as ranking defects or index drift, and future interfaces will invent ad hoc diagnostics.

comments.author: quangdang46
comments.text:
## Background
Cache work is easy to smuggle in as 'just performance', but in membrain caches are derived accelerators and must never become hidden sources of truth.

## Why this issue exists
`docs/CACHE_AND_PREFETCH.md` enumerates several cache families and warming strategies, but the earlier backlog mostly implied them across retrieval, storage, and ops issues. This task gives them one canonical contract.

## Source Map
- docs/CACHE_AND_PREFETCH.md
- docs/PLAN.md Sections 5, 6, and 7
- docs/MCP_API.md

## Future-Self Notes
- Prefer one shared invalidation vocabulary over bespoke rules per cache.
- If a cache cannot explain staleness, disable or fallback behavior, and ownership boundaries, it is not production-ready.
- Keep prefetch optional and bounded; recall correctness must not depend on warm state.

title: Retrieval cache subtask: define cache families, namespace-aware keys, and version invalidation
description:

- The supported cache families and their ownership boundaries are explicit enough that future contributors know what is authoritative versus disposable.
- Key composition includes the scope and version inputs needed to avoid cross-namespace leakage and stale reuse after schema, policy, or index changes.
- Invalidation and rebuild triggers are explicit for writes, reconsolidation, forgetting, repair, migration, and policy updates.

comments.author: quangdang46
comments.text:
## Expanded Readiness-Gate Notes for Distribution
Measured demand should include more than size. Require evidence about workload shape, latency pressure, operator pain, rebuild windows, and isolation constraints.

### Candidate evidence categories
- sustained benchmark pressure beyond current budgets,
- unmanageable maintenance windows or rebuild durations,
- unacceptable namespace/workspace locality imbalance,
- proven limits in single-node recovery or compaction operations,
- clear operator burden that simpler optimizations cannot solve.

### Future-Self Notes
Use this issue to protect the project from scale-theater. Distribution should only happen when core correctness, observability, and repairability are already credible.
